title: Term Insurance Guide — Cheapest Life Cover for Your Family
meta_description: Term insurance guide 2026 — ₹1 crore cover for ₹500-1,500/month. How much cover you need, best plans, claim ratio, riders, and when to buy.

hero.one_liner: ₹1 crore life insurance for just ₹500-1,500/month — the cheapest way to financially protect your family against the worst-case scenario

sections.heading: Overview
content: Term insurance is the simplest and cheapest form of life insurance available. You pay a small premium every year, and if you die during the policy term, your family (nominee) receives the full sum assured — say ₹1 crore.
If you survive the term (say 30 years), you get nothing back. No maturity benefit, no cash value, no investment component.
It's pure protection — and that's exactly why it's so incredibly cheap.

For a 30-year-old non-smoking male, a ₹1 crore term plan from a top insurer costs approximately ₹8,000-15,000/year (₹700-1,250/month). Compare this with a traditional LIC endowment plan where you'd pay ₹80,000-1,00,000/year for the same ₹1 crore cover — plus the endowment gives you only 5-6% returns on the investment component.
Term insurance gives you 10-15× the cover at 1/10th the cost. This is why every financial expert calls term insurance the 'first financial product everyone should buy'.

Every financial advisor, SEBI-registered advisor, and personal finance expert recommends term insurance as the FIRST financial product every earning adult should buy. If you have any financial dependents (spouse, children, parents, siblings relying on your income), you need term insurance.
The formula: buy 10-15× your annual income in term cover.

Claim settlement ratios of major term insurers are 97-99% — meaning 97-99 out of every 100 claims are paid. The 1-3% rejections are almost always due to non-disclosure of pre-existing conditions or fraud.

If you disclose all health conditions honestly during application, your claim will be paid. Term insurance claims are among the fastest-settling in the insurance industry — typically within 15-30 days.

sections.heading: How Much Term Cover Do You Need? — The Formula
content:
Method 1 — Income Multiplier

Cover = 10-15× annual income. If you earn ₹8 lakh/year, buy ₹80L-1.2Cr cover.
This ensures your family can sustain their lifestyle for 10-15 years while adjusting to your absence and finding new income sources.

Method 2 — Expense-Based (more accurate)

Add up: (1) Annual household expenses × remaining working years. If expenses are ₹5L/year and you have 30 working years left = ₹1.5 Cr. (2) Outstanding loans (home loan, car loan, personal loan).

If home loan = ₹30L. (3) Children's future education costs (₹20-50L per child for graduation). (4) Children's marriage fund (₹10-20L per child). (5) Emergency fund (6 months expenses = ₹25L if monthly spending is ₹5L). Total need: ₹1.5Cr + ₹30L + ₹40L + ₹20L + ₹25L = ₹2.65 Cr.
Subtract: existing savings (₹40L), EPF/NPS corpus (₹50L). Required cover: ₹2.65Cr - ₹90L = ₹1.75Cr → Round up to ₹2Cr.

Don't under-insure to save premium

The difference between ₹1Cr and ₹2Cr cover is only ₹3,000-5,000/year extra (0.3-0.4% of annual income for most). Your family's financial security and freedom from stress is worth that cost.
A ₹1Cr cover is insufficient if your family needs ₹1.5Cr to sustain.

sections.heading: Top Term Insurance Plans to Consider (2026)
content: When choosing a term plan, prioritize: (1) Claim Settlement Ratio (CSR) — should be 97%+, (2) Company financial strength and track record, (3) Premium affordability, (4) Rider options (critical illness, accidental death, premium waiver).

Top-rated term plans by claim ratio and reputation: HDFC Life Click 2 Protect (CSR: 99%+, affordable, clean track record), ICICI Pru iProtect Smart (CSR: 98%+, good rider options, competitive pricing), Max Life Smart Secure Plus (CSR: 99%+, strong customer support), Tata AIA Sampoorna Raksha (CSR: 98%+, good for non-smokers), LIC e-Term (CSR: 98%+, government-backed, slightly expensive), Canara HSBC eProtect (CSR: 99%+, new but emerging).

Buy online for lowest premium — online term plans are 20-40% cheaper than offline plans from the same insurer because the distribution cost is nil. The policy is identical; only the commission structure is different.
Online buying through PolicyBazaar, CoverFox, or directly from insurer websites gives you the best rates.
Avoid plans with return of premium (TROP) features unless you're very wealthy. They cost 40-60% more premium for the same cover, and the returned premium doesn't even beat inflation after 30 years.
Example: ₹1Cr TROP plan = ₹16,000/year vs ₹8,000/year for regular plan. Over 30 years: TROP costs ₹4.8L, regular costs ₹2.4L.
Even investing the ₹8,000/year difference in a 12% SIP gives ₹1.5Cr — more than the ₹4.8L returned.

sections.heading: When to Buy — Age vs Premium Impact
Important Riders to Consider
content:
Critical Illness Rider (highly recommended)

Pays a lump sum (₹25-50L) if you're diagnosed with a covered critical illness — cancer, heart attack, stroke, kidney failure, major organ transplant, paralysis, etc. This payout is IN ADDITION to the death benefit.
Covers 30-40 critical illnesses depending on the plan. Cost: ₹1,500-3,000/year extra for ₹50L CI cover.
Benefit: If you're diagnosed with cancer and need ₹30L treatment, you get the money immediately from CI rider without affecting your death benefit. Your family still gets ₹1Cr if you pass away.

Accidental Death Benefit Rider

Pays an additional amount (₹50L-1Cr) if death is due to accident (car crash, fall, drowning, etc.). Some plans offer double the base cover for accidental death.
Cost: ₹500-1,000/year extra. Benefit: Cover is highest for the most common cause of death in younger people — accidents.

Waiver of Premium Rider

If you become permanently disabled and can't work, the insurer waives all future premiums — your policy continues without payment. Cost: ₹1,000-2,000/year extra.
Benefit: If you meet an accident that causes permanent disability, you don't have to worry about paying premiums.

Conversion Rider

Allows converting your term plan to a whole life or endowment plan without re-underwriting. Useful if you want to switch strategies later.
Cost: Usually ₹500-1,000/year. Skip riders that inflate premium significantly beyond necessity.
The base term cover is the most important thing. Add Critical Illness if affordable — it's the most valuable rider for working-age individuals.

steps.title: Calculate cover needed (10-15× income)
steps.description: Use the formula: Add family's annual living cost × years remaining till retirement + child's education (₹20-50L per child) + child's marriage (₹10-20L per child) + outstanding loans + emergency fund. Subtract: savings + EPF/NPS balance. The gap = required cover.

steps.title: Compare plans online
steps.description: Use PolicyBazaar, CoverFox, or InsureMe. Compare premiums, features, riders, and claim ratios for the same cover amount across 5-10 insurers. Buy online for lowest premium.

steps.title: Fill application with complete and honest health disclosure
steps.description: Disclose ALL health conditions, medications, surgeries, family history of diseases, smoking/drinking habits, occupational hazards. Non-disclosure is the #1 reason for claim rejection. Honesty protects your family — pre-existing conditions are covered after an initial period.

steps.title: Complete medical exam if required
steps.description: For ₹50L+ cover, the insurer sends a paramedic to your home for blood test, urine test, BP check, BMI. Results go directly to insurer (not visible to anyone). For ₹25L-50L, sometimes phone-based medical underwriting is done.

steps.title: Pay first premium and receive policy
steps.description: After medical underwriting (1-2 weeks), if approved, pay the first premium. Policy document is emailed. Keep your nominee informed about the policy — tell them your insurer name, policy number, and how to file a claim.

faqs.question: I already have LIC policy. Do I still need term insurance?
faqs.answer: Almost certainly yes. Most LIC endowment/money-back policies provide only ₹5-15L cover — nowhere near the 10-15× income (₹80L-1.5Cr) you need. A ₹10L LIC policy is dangerously insufficient if your family needs ₹1.5Cr to sustain. Buy a separate ₹1-2Cr term plan for adequate cover. You can keep the LIC policy for its maturity value — it's not harmful, just insufficient.

faqs.question: Is online term insurance safe to buy?
faqs.answer: Yes, completely. Online term plans are issued by the same IRDAI-regulated insurance companies as offline plans. The policy terms, claim process, and legal protections are identical. Online plans are cheaper only because they save on agent commission (15-30% of premium). The insurer is equally obligated to honor claims — online vs offline makes no difference to your protection.

faqs.question: What is Return of Premium (TROP) term plan?
faqs.answer: TROP returns all paid premiums if you survive the policy term. Sounds attractive but costs 40-60% more premium. Example: Regular ₹1Cr term plan at ₹10,000/year = ₹3L premium over 30 years. TROP costs ₹16,000/year = ₹4.8L premium. Difference = ₹1.8L. If you invest that ₹6,000/year difference in a 12% SIP for 30 years, you'd have ₹15+ lakh — far more than the ₹4.8L premium returned. Strategy: Skip TROP, buy regular term, invest the savings in SIP.

faqs.question: My company provides ₹25 lakh group insurance. Is that enough?
faqs.answer: No. Group insurance has two problems: (1) It ends when you leave the company — if you change jobs, quit, or are laid off, you lose cover instantly. You might be without insurance during the job transition. (2) ₹25L is inadequate for most families' needs. Buy personal term insurance for your core protection. Treat company insurance as a bonus that supplements your personal plan.

faqs.question: What happens if I miss a premium payment?
faqs.answer: Most term plans have a 30-day grace period after the due date. Pay within grace period — coverage continues uninterrupted. Premium due on Jan 1? Grace till Jan 30. If not paid within grace, the policy lapses. You can revive it within 2-5 years (revival period varies) by paying all pending premiums + late fees + possibly a fresh medical exam. After the revival period (usually 5 years), the policy terminates permanently and cannot be revived.

faqs.question: Can I have multiple term insurance policies?
faqs.answer: Yes. There's no limit on the number of policies from different insurers. Many people have 2-3 term plans (e.g., ₹50L from one insurer, ₹75L from another) for diversification and to ensure claims are paid even if one insurer faces issues. The total claim payout from all policies goes to your nominees. Just ensure the total cover is justified by your financial needs — insurers check during underwriting to prevent moral hazard (over-insurance).

disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only. Consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
